questions.prompt: Which best describes the purpose of software testing?
options: To prove the software has no bugs
To gather information about quality and risk to inform a ship decision
To click through every screen at least once
To approve the release on behalf of the business
questions.explanation: Testing produces information about quality and risk; the ship/no-ship decision is made by stakeholders using that information.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between QA and QC?
options: They are two names for the same activity
QA improves the process to prevent defects; QC checks the product for defects
QC is done by developers, QA by managers only
QA is manual, QC is automated
questions.explanation: QA is process-focused (prevent defects); QC is product-focused (detect defects). Testing is a QC activity.

questions.prompt: "Testing can show the presence of bugs, but not their absence." This means:
options: Testing is pointless
Passing tests guarantee the software is bug-free
Testing reduces uncertainty but can never prove perfection
You should only test until the first bug is found
questions.explanation: No finite set of tests covers every input and state, so testing lowers risk without ever guaranteeing zero defects.

questions.prompt: On a release dashboard, which item represents the risk a tester most needs to surface?
options: A feature tested and passing
A feature that was never tested (unknown)
A feature that is out of scope
A feature the team already removed
questions.explanation: Untested areas are unpriced risk. Surfacing the unknowns is often more valuable than confirming the knowns.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is the cleanest expression of a potential bug?
options: The app is bad
Checkout feels slow sometimes
When I submitted an expired card, I expected an error but the order was placed
Someone should look at payments
questions.explanation: Expected-vs-actual with a concrete trigger is the atom of a bug report — specific, reproducible, and verifiable.
